county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Burt County formed 1854 as original Nebraska Territory county. County seat is Tekamah. Missouri River county. Borders the Omaha Indian Reservation (now Thurston County). Among the earliest Nebraska counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Burt County (formed 1854, seat Tekamah) is one of the original Nebraska Territory counties along the Missouri River. Records are at the courthouse in Tekamah. Being an original county, Burt has some of the earliest records in the state. The Burt County Museum in Tekamah preserves local historical materials. Early settlement included significant interaction with the Omaha tribe, whose reservation borders Burt County (now in Thurston County). Check NARA Kansas City for Omaha Agency records if researching Native American families. Land records include early pre-emption claims and homesteads -- BLM GLO records are essential. FamilySearch has microfilmed Burt County records. History Nebraska holds territorial-era documents. The 1855-1856 Nebraska territorial census and the 1885 state census are available. German and Scandinavian immigrants were prominent from the 1860s onward.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Burt County. County Register of Deeds in Tekamah holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.source_name: Burt County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Burt County Register of Deeds, Tekamah
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1854 to present. Nebraska is a public land state; initial federal patents are at BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov). Subsequent transfers recorded at the county Register of Deeds. Includes deeds, mortgages, liens, and plats.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records intact from county formation

source_profiles.source_name: Burt County Marriage Records -- County Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from 1854. Nebraska marriage licenses issued by county judges (County Court). State registration began 1909. Earlier marriages recorded at county level. FamilySearch has indexed many Nebraska marriages.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1854. Nebraska probate is handled by the County Court. Records include wills, letters of administration, estate inventories, guardianship files, and adoption records.

Territorial censuses (1854-1856, 1860) exist for early counties. Available through FamilySearch and Ancestry.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Butler County formed 1856 from unorganized territory. County seat is David City. Strong Czech/Bohemian settlement. David City and surrounding areas attracted Czech immigrants from the 1860s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Butler County (formed 1856, seat David City) records are at the courthouse in David City. Butler County has one of Nebraska's strongest Czech/Bohemian communities -- David City and surrounding areas attracted large numbers of Czech immigrants from the 1860s onward. Czech Catholic and Czech Brethren church records are critical for family research; check diocesan archives (Archdiocese of Omaha) and FamilySearch. The ZCBJ (Zapadni Cesko-Bratrska Jednota, Western Bohemian Fraternal Association) had active lodges. Homestead entries at BLM GLO and county deed records at the Register of Deeds are primary land sources. History Nebraska in Lincoln holds Czech heritage collections. The 1885 state census is available. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Butler County Historical Society may have family files and photographs.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Butler County. County Register of Deeds in David City holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Cass County formed 1854 as original Nebraska Territory county. County seat is Plattsmouth. Missouri River county. Early railroad center (Burlington and Missouri River Railroad).
county_metadata.research_tips: Cass County (formed 1854, seat Plattsmouth) is one of the original Nebraska Territory counties along the Missouri River. Records are at the courthouse in Plattsmouth. The Cass County Historical Society Museum in Plattsmouth holds local genealogical materials. Plattsmouth was an important Missouri River town and early railroad center -- the Burlington and Missouri River Railroad headquartered operations here. Being one of the earliest counties, Cass has records dating to the territorial period (1854-1867). Pre-emption and homestead entries at BLM GLO document early settlers. The territorial and 1885 state censuses are available. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of Cass County deed, probate, marriage, and court records. German, Irish, and native-born American settlers from Iowa and Missouri were predominant. Newspapers from Plattsmouth (1860s onward) at History Nebraska are valuable for obituaries and legal notices.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Cass County. County Register of Deeds in Plattsmouth holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Cedar County formed 1857 from Dixon and Pierce counties. County seat is Hartington. Strong German Catholic settlement. Northeast Nebraska. Borders the Santee Sioux Reservation area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cedar County (formed 1857, seat Hartington) records are at the courthouse in Hartington. Cedar County attracted strong German Catholic settlement, especially around Hartington, Bow Valley, and St. Helena. Catholic parish records (Archdiocese of Omaha) are essential for baptisms, marriages, and burials. The Cedar County Historical Society maintains local materials. Homestead entries dominate early land records -- check BLM GLO. Cedar County borders the Missouri River and the Santee Sioux Reservation (Knox County) -- researchers with Native American connections should check NARA Kansas City. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The 1885 state census and federal censuses (1860-1950) are available. History Nebraska in Lincoln has supplementary records. Czech and Irish settlers also contributed to Cedar County's ethnic mix.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Cedar County. County Register of Deeds in Hartington holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Chase County formed 1873 from unorganized territory. County seat is Imperial. Southwestern Nebraska along the Republican River valley. Homesteading and farming.
county_metadata.research_tips: Chase County (formed 1873, seat Imperial) in southwestern Nebraska was carved from unorganized territory. Records are at the courthouse in Imperial. Settlement came primarily through homesteading in the 1880s-1890s. Chase County's location along the Republican River valley influenced agricultural development. BLM GLO records document homestead and pre-emption entries. The Burlington Railroad brought settlers from the east. German, Scandinavian, and Anglo-American homesteaders were the primary groups. FamilySearch has limited microfilm. The Chase County Historical Society may have local materials. History Nebraska in Lincoln is the backup repository. The 1885 state census may have early enumeration. Chase County's proximity to Colorado and Kansas means some families crossed state lines -- check neighboring county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Chase County. County Register of Deeds in Imperial holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Cherry County formed 1883 from unorganized territory. County seat is Valentine. Nebraska's largest county by area (~6,000 sq mi). Sandhills cattle ranching. Fort Niobrara (1880-1906). Kinkaid Act homesteads.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cherry County (formed 1883, seat Valentine) is the largest county in Nebraska by area (nearly 6,000 square miles) and is located in the Sandhills. Records are at the courthouse in Valentine. Despite its vast size, Cherry County has always had a small population focused on cattle ranching. The Kinkaid Act (1904) was particularly important here, allowing 640-acre homesteads. BLM GLO records are essential for land entries. Valentine is near the former Fort Niobrara military reservation -- military records at NARA may be relevant. The Cherry County Historical Society in Valentine preserves local materials. Niobrara River and Sandhills ecology shaped settlement. FamilySearch has limited microfilm. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. Federal censuses from 1890 (destroyed) onward and the 1885 state census are the primary population records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Cherry County. County Register of Deeds in Valentine holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.source_name: Kinkaid Act Homestead Records -- Cherry County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kinkaid Act (1904) homestead records for Cherry County. The Kinkaid Act allowed 640-acre homestead claims in the Sandhills and other western Nebraska counties, recognizing that 160 acres was insufficient for ranching in semi-arid grasslands. Original case files at NARA; patents at BLM GLO.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Military records for Fort Niobrara (1880-1906). Post returns, muster rolls, hospital records, and personnel files at NARA. History Nebraska may hold supplementary materials. Military post records document soldiers, dependents, civilian employees, and local interactions.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Cheyenne County formed 1867 from unorganized territory. County seat is Sidney. Panhandle county on the Union Pacific mainline. Fort Sidney (1867-1894). Supply point for Black Hills gold rush.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cheyenne County (formed 1867, seat Sidney) is a Panhandle county along the Union Pacific mainline. Records are at the courthouse in Sidney. Sidney was a major Union Pacific Railroad town and supply point for the Black Hills gold rush (1870s). The Cheyenne County Historical Society preserves local materials. Fort Sidney (1867-1894) military records are at NARA. Homestead, pre-emption, and Timber Culture Act entries at BLM GLO document settlers. Cheyenne County was parent to several western Panhandle counties (Banner, Deuel, Kimball). German-Russian and Scandinavian immigrants were numerous. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The 1885 state census is available. History Nebraska in Lincoln holds territorial and early statehood records. Sidney's newspapers from the 1870s are valuable sources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Cheyenne County. County Register of Deeds in Sidney holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Clay County formed 1855 as original Nebraska Territory county. County seat is Clay Center. South-central Nebraska. Little Blue River valley settlement. Near the Oregon and California Trail corridor.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clay County (formed 1855, seat Clay Center) is one of the original Nebraska Territory counties in south-central Nebraska. Records are at the courthouse in Clay Center. Clay County's location along the Little Blue River attracted early settlement. Homestead entries are documented at BLM GLO. The Harvard area had notable Scandinavian settlement. German, Swedish, and Anglo-American families predominated. The Clay County Historical Society may have local materials. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The territorial censuses and 1885 state census are available. History Nebraska in Lincoln holds supplementary records. Clay County is near the Oregon and California Trail corridor -- trail-related records may be relevant for very early research. Burlington and Missouri River Railroad land sale records may document early purchasers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Clay County. County Register of Deeds in Clay Center holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from 1855. Nebraska marriage licenses issued by county judges (County Court). State registration began 1909. Earlier marriages recorded at county level. FamilySearch has indexed many Nebraska marriages.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Colfax County formed 1869 from Platte County. County seat is Schuyler. Strong Polish and Czech/Bohemian settlement. Platte Valley county on the Union Pacific Railroad.
county_metadata.research_tips: Colfax County (formed 1869, seat Schuyler) records are at the courthouse in Schuyler. Colfax County is notable for its strong Polish community in and around the Schuyler and Tarnov area -- Polish Catholic parish records are essential sources. The county also has significant Czech/Bohemian and Irish settlement. Catholic church records (Archdiocese of Omaha) document baptisms, marriages, and burials. The Polish Heritage Center and various fraternal organizations (PNA, PRCUA) may have records. Homestead entries at BLM GLO document original settlers. FamilySearch has microfilmed Colfax County records. The 1885 state census is available. History Nebraska holds related materials. Schuyler's meatpacking industry later attracted Hispanic/Latino workers. The Platte Valley location made transportation accessible via the Union Pacific Railroad.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Colfax County. County Register of Deeds in Schuyler holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.repository: Union Pacific Historical Collection / History Nebraska / Nebraska State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Union Pacific Railroad land grant and sale records for Colfax County. The UP received alternate sections of land along its right-of-way. Land department records document sales to settlers, town lot sales, and colony arrangements. UP Historical Collection at History Nebraska.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Cuming County formed 1855 from Burt County. County seat is West Point. Strong German Catholic heritage. Eastern Nebraska. Elkhorn River valley.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cuming County (formed 1855, seat West Point) is an eastern Nebraska county with records at the courthouse in West Point. Cuming County has strong German Catholic heritage -- the West Point and Beemer areas attracted German immigrants from the 1860s onward. Catholic parish records (Archdiocese of Omaha) and German Lutheran records are critical sources. The Cuming County Historical Society preserves local materials. Homestead and pre-emption entries at BLM GLO document land acquisition. The Elkhorn River valley influenced settlement. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The territorial and 1885 state censuses are available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. German-language newspapers published in West Point are a unique source for this community.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Cuming County. County Register of Deeds in West Point holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.
